questions.question: Explain the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm with region codes.
keyPoints: Define 4-bit region code with bits for top(1000), bottom(0100), right(0010), left(0001)
Explain trivial accept when both codes are 0000
Explain trivial reject when AND of codes is non-zero
Describe the subdivision and intersection calculation process

questions.question: What are the advantages of Liang-Barsky algorithm over Cohen-Sutherland?
keyPoints: Parametric approach is more efficient for rectangular windows
Requires fewer calculations per clipping operation
Easily extends to 3D clipping
Computes both entry and exit parameters simultaneously

questions.question: Derive the parametric equations used in Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithm.
keyPoints: x = x1 + t(x2-x1) for 0≤t≤1
y = y1 + t(y2-y1) for 0≤t≤1
Explain p and q values for each boundary
Show inequality constraints for each edge

questions.question: Differentiate between clip window and viewport in computer graphics.
keyPoints: Clip window is defined in world/normalized coordinates
Viewport is the display area on output device
Clip window defines what to render; viewport defines where to display
Window-to-viewport transformation maps world coordinates to screen coordinates

questions.question: Explain the trivial accept and trivial reject cases in Cohen-Sutherland algorithm.
keyPoints: Trivial accept: both endpoints inside (code 0000) - entire line visible
Trivial reject: both endpoints in same outside region (AND ≠ 0) - line completely outside
If neither case applies, need to compute intersections
Explain how subdivision works with endpoints and intersections

questions.question: Describe the Cyrus-Beck algorithm and its for convex clip regions.
keyPoints: Uses parametric representation like Liang-Barsky
Works with any convex polygon using normal vectors
Calculates dot products to determine entry/exit at each edge
Finds intersection with each edge and determines visible portion

questions.question: Clipping is essential in computer graphics. Justify this statement.
keyPoints: Prevents rendering objects outside display area
Reduces memory and computation overhead
Enables efficient rendering pipeline
Allows windowing and view operations
Prevents undefined operations when drawing outside boundaries

questions.question: Explain the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm in detail. Apply the algorithm to clip line segment from (50,10) to (80,70) against clip window with boundaries xmin=40, xmax=60, ymin=20, ymax=50.
keyPoints: Explain 4-bit region code assignment for each endpoint
Show calculations for endpoint codes
Determine trivial accept/reject or need for clipping
Calculate intersection points with each edge crossed
Show final clipped line segment coordinates
Verify result makes sense

questions.question: Explain Liang-Barsky parametric line clipping algorithm. How does it determine if a line segment is visible, partially visible, or invisible? Derive the final clipped coordinates.
keyPoints: Explain parametric form of line segment
Define p and q parameters for all four edges
Show how t0 (entry) and t1 (exit) are computed
Explain conditions for visible/partially visible/invisible
Compare with Cohen-Sutherland advantages
Give example demonstrating algorithm

questions.question: Compare Cohen-Sutherland, Liang-Barsky, and Cyrus-Beck line clipping algorithms. Under what circumstances would you choose each algorithm?
keyPoints: Cohen-Sutherland: uses region codes, good for trivial rejection, simple implementation
Liang-Barsky: parametric, efficient for rectangles, fewer multiplications
Cyrus-Beck: works for convex polygons, uses normal vectors
Use Cohen-Sutherland when many lines can be trivially rejected
Use Liang-Barsky for rectangular windows with many lines needing clipping
Use Cyrus-Beck for non-rectangular convex clip regions

questions.question: Describe the step-by-step procedure of Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm. For a line from P1(10,10) to P2(90,90), determine which cases apply for clip window xmin=30, xmax=70, ymin=30, ymax=70.
keyPoints: Step 1: Compute 4-bit region codes for both endpoints
Step 2: Check trivial accept (both 0000)
Step 3: Check trivial reject (AND ≠ 0)
Step 4: For non-trivial case, find intersection with window edges
Step 5: Subdivide line and repeat until fully clipped
Calculate region codes: P1=0101 (below+left), P2=1010 (above+right)
Explain this requires clipping, compute intersections
